item.title: Make a difference for cats and dogs in need!
item.description:
Thursday, March 26, 2026 at 12 AM

Location: Pawsitive Alliance, 14150 NE 20th St. F1-233, Bellevue, WA

Are you interested in using your time, talent, and energy to support the vision of a healthy and happy home for every cat and dog in Washington? If yes, then please consider joining our Board! Pawsitive Alliance, a volunteer driven tax-exempt nonprofit organization, is having an open call for Board Members. Founded in 2005, the mission of Pawsitive Alliance is to help end the euthanasia of adoptable dogs and cats in Washington by increasing adoptions, supporting spay and neuter programs, and improving pet retention. Pawsitive Alliance advances are mission through our three programs: -  Adoption program: \#WhyNotMePets campaign, highlighting long-term homeless pets, and supporting adoption events to help homeless finds homes -  Spay/Neuter: providing vouchers and financial and logistical support to increase surgeries and access to end pet overpopulation and suffering -  Pet retention: collecting and distributing pet food and supplies and offering pet ID tags and microchips to keep pets thriving with their caretakers Our programs and activities prioritize increasing services for pets, people, and places across Washington state with few resources. Pawsitive Alliance’s biggest strengths are marketing, organizing, fundraising, and collaborating.  We work with more than 50 human and animal-welfare partners each year. Since 2005, we have helped over 8,000 animals find homes, delivered over 170,000 pounds of donated pet food, and facilitated more than 11,000 spay and neuter surgeries. The Pawsitive Alliance Board is a hands-on group of committed individuals, investing their time and resources to support our mission.  As we celebrate our 20th anniversary in 2025, and look to the future, we are looking to add individuals from diverse backgrounds to join the Board and help the organization’s continued success and growth.  Ideal candidates are committed to our mission, can commit at least 10-15 per month to the organization, are able to represent the organization in the community, are ready to engage in strategy setting, fundraising and networking, and bring a well-rounded set of skills to a unique and hard-working team.​ Pawsitive Alliance is always interested in bringing greater diversity and new areas of expertise, energy, and skills to the Board. For more information on the organization, refer to our website at www.pawsitivealliance.org.  Please fill out our Board Application if you share our passion for helping to end pet homelessness in Washington and are interested in serving and helping end pet overpopulation. Those interested in applying should email our Board Recruitment Committee at info@pawsitivealliance.org.

From SEAtoday - Community & Civic

Event details

item.title: Figure/Ground: New Criteria
item.description:
Thursday, March 26, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Henry Art Gallery, 15th Ave NE & 41st St, Seattle, WA

Figure/Ground: New Criteria is inspired by cultural historian Sarah Lewis’s concept of “groundwork aesthetics,” exploring figure-ground relationships and their connection to social tension. It builds on Lewis’s work, which—set against the expansion of Stand Your Ground laws across the United States since 2005, laws that broadly define rights to use lethal self-defense against perceived threats—describes a range of artistic interventions that reimagine “the literal and figurative meaning of ground.” Primarily featuring works from the Henry collection created in the twenty-first century, Figure/Ground reflects a period in which hard-won civil rights and claims to self-determination have been eroded across the US, disproportionately affecting Black, Brown, LGBTQ\+, and other marginalized communities.

From SEAtoday - Community & Civic

Event details

item.title: Beyond Mysticism: The Modern Northwest
item.description:
Thursday, March 26, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

image: Drift No. 2, 1936, Malcolm M. Roberts, American, 1913-1990, tempera on board, 25 1/2 x 20 5/8 in., Seattle Art Museum, Eugene Fuller Memorial Collection, 37.103, © Malcolm M. Roberts, photo: Scott Leen. Inspired by the region’s natural beauty and rapidly changing city, this exhibition showcases artists who told the raw story of Seattle in the 20th century. By sharing the Pacific Northwest’s perspective through trending art styles—including Social Realism, Surrealism, and Abstract Expressionism—these artists offered a unique take on Modernist art and challenged the “mystic” label ascribed to their works. Spearheading the movement were local icons Mark Tobey, Kenneth Callahan, Guy Anderson, and Morris Graves—but the range and breadth of Pacific Northwest art goes beyond the “Big Four” of the Northwest School. Featuring more than 150 pieces, this exhibition showcases works that investigate the complexities of daily life in the region. Whether applying an ecocritical lens or producing dreamy and uncanny visuals, these artists expressed the duality of the natural world and city life to warn of the environmental and social impacts of industrialization. From Malcolm Roberts to Kenjiro Nomura, learn how Northwest artists applied their regional perspective to broader artistic developments to create a new form of American Modernism. The exhibition is accompanied by a fully illustrated catalogue authored by Theresa Papanikolas, SAM’s Ann M. Barwick Curator of American Art.

From SEAtoday - Community & Civic

Event details

item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Thursday, March 26, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

From SEAtoday - Community & Civic

Event details

item.title: Samantha Yun Wall: What We Leave Behind
item.description:
Thursday, March 26, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Samantha Yun Wall’s new paintings use overlapping silhouettes of female figures as portals to unknown spaces and different temporal realms. Impetus for the new body of work is a Korean folk tale in which the Pasque flower is symbolic of a grandmother who passed away without the loving care of her grandchildren. It is a story of melancholy, loss, and remembrance. The delicate hair on the flower’s stem differentiates it, and the artist gives the plant a surreal aspect in some of her paintings, replacing the flower’s center with a watchful eye.

From SEAtoday - Community & Civic

Event details

item.title: Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light
item.description:
Thursday, March 26, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light invites you to become part of the art. Agha, a Pakistani American artist, animates spaces with her large-scale sculptural installations. Suspended from the ceiling, Agha’s steel cubes are laser-cut with intricate designs that project geometric shadows onto the visitor. She draws on both the light and dark of her own life, using South Asian art practices to convey the gender discrimination she faced growing up as a young girl in Pakistan. An in-depth exploration of Islamic architecture, art, and identity, this is the first solo exhibition of a Pakistani American artist in SAM’s 90-year history. Agha is carving a name out for herself in the art world—visit the Seattle Asian Art Museum for an immersive and illuminating experience.

From SEAtoday - Community & Civic

Event details

item.title: Kameelah Janan Rasheed: we leak, we exceed
item.description:
Thursday, March 26, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Henry Art Gallery, 15th Ave NE & 41st St, Seattle, WA

Kameelah Janan Rasheed \(b. 1985, East Palo Alto, CA; lives and works in Brooklyn, NY\) is a self-described “learner” who creates multi-media works that explore the poetics-pleasures-politics of Black knowledge production, information technologies, \[un\]learning, and belief formation. She often integrates found images and words alongside her own writing to create collaged wall installations and experimental video works. Across her work, Rasheed investigates the revision and life cycles of text, embracing expanded and embodied forms of writing and reading. we leak, we exceed will activate the unique volume and multiple vantage points of the Henry’s double-height gallery, drawing together threads from physics, Black critical thought, and information theory to create an immersive environment that interrogates the spatial and social implications of compression. A common process used in data storage, spatial organization, and information systems, compression abbreviates and collapses complex ideas into more simplified forms. Rasheed questions the way compression comes at the cost of nuance and creates unrecoverable losses. She draws parallels between the compression of information and the containment of people, both physically and through the structuring and defining of identities. Through a network of video, sound, and architectural mark-making, Rasheed proposes alternatively what she calls “an embrace of Black excess and expansion” as a liberatory practice.

From SEAtoday - Community & Civic

item.title: REACH - Connecting people experiencing homelessness with help and resources
item.description:
Thursday, March 26, 2026 at 10:30 AM

Thursday, March 26, 2026, 10:30am - 12:30pm REACH will be at the library to get to know our patrons and connect them to resources that they may need. Location Link: International District/Chinatown Branch Registration Required: Registration is not required. Detailed Description: REACH builds relationships with people experiencing homelessness andconnects them to the help that they need- everything from food and clothing to medical care, shelter, and mental health and/or substance use treatment., They respond to each client's unique social and clinical needs. Their work embraces the worth and humanity of the people we serve, partnering with them to effectively navigate the system., Thanks to our partner REACH. Contact Us: If you have questions about this event, Ask Us or call 206-386-4636. ADA Accommodations: We can provide accommodations for people with disabilities at Library events. Please contactLEAP at least seven days before the event to request accommodations.All Library programs are free and open to the public.

Event details

item.title: Boren Banner Series: Camille Trautman
item.description:
Thursday, March 26, 2026 at 11 AM

Location: Frye Art Museum, 704 Terry Ave, Seattle, WA

Every photograph is a reminder that the act of framing is never neutral. In Camille Trautman’s first solo museum exhibition in their hometown, the Seattle-born Duwamish artist uses photography and video to challenge colonial narratives and counter Indigenous erasure. The exhibition presents selections from their ongoing series The North American LCD—spectral self-portraits staged in varied natural landscapes. Rooted in Trautman’s process of coming out as transgender, the images show the artist’s body partially obscured by large LCD screens. The series examines how both landscape photography and digital media can shape or distort cultural identity, often by promoting or denying visibility.

From SEAtoday - Community & Civic

item.title: Scott Gibson - Visual Symphony II
item.description:
Thursday, March 26, 2026 at 12 PM

Location: Gray Sky Gallery, 320 First Ave. S., Seattle, WA

In March, Visual Symphony II Scott Gibson invites viewers into a world where music and art collide. Each work is an exploration of the vivid, sensory experience of sound translating into vibrant, emotional visuals. As an artist with synesthesia each work expresses Scott’s personal perception of what music looks like. Scott uses resin techniques that he has pioneered and pushes the boundaries of this medium.

item.title: North End Ecosocialism Reading Group
item.description:
Thursday, March 26, 2026 at 7 PM

Location: Armistice Coffee Roosevelt\, 6717 Roosevelt Ave NE\, Seattle\, WA

Join us at Armistice Coffee Roosevelt\, to discuss the book Practical Radicals: Seven Strategies to Change the World\, by Deepak Bhargava and Stephanie Luce\, through the end of Chapter 11. \nYou don’t have to live in the north end\, and you can feel free to join the conversation even if you haven’t had time to read.  Our biweekly discussions seek to inform strategies for winning a democratic socialist society that thrives ecologically. All are welcome! \nWe sit outdoors in a breezeway–dress warm. Any questions or difficulty getting the book\, please email greennewdeal@seattledsa.org.

item.title: Solidarity Dinner: South Seattle
item.description:

item.title: Figure/Ground: New Criteria
item.description:
Saturday, March 28, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Henry Art Gallery, 15th Ave NE & 41st St, Seattle, WA

Figure/Ground: New Criteria is inspired by cultural historian Sarah Lewis’s concept of “groundwork aesthetics,” exploring figure-ground relationships and their connection to social tension. It builds on Lewis’s work, which—set against the expansion of Stand Your Ground laws across the United States since 2005, laws that broadly define rights to use lethal self-defense against perceived threats—describes a range of artistic interventions that reimagine “the literal and figurative meaning of ground.” Primarily featuring works from the Henry collection created in the twenty-first century, Figure/Ground reflects a period in which hard-won civil rights and claims to self-determination have been eroded across the US, disproportionately affecting Black, Brown, LGBTQ\+, and other marginalized communities.

From SEAtoday - Community & Civic

Event details

item.title: Beyond Mysticism: The Modern Northwest
item.description:
Saturday, March 28, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

image: Drift No. 2, 1936, Malcolm M. Roberts, American, 1913-1990, tempera on board, 25 1/2 x 20 5/8 in., Seattle Art Museum, Eugene Fuller Memorial Collection, 37.103, © Malcolm M. Roberts, photo: Scott Leen. Inspired by the region’s natural beauty and rapidly changing city, this exhibition showcases artists who told the raw story of Seattle in the 20th century. By sharing the Pacific Northwest’s perspective through trending art styles—including Social Realism, Surrealism, and Abstract Expressionism—these artists offered a unique take on Modernist art and challenged the “mystic” label ascribed to their works. Spearheading the movement were local icons Mark Tobey, Kenneth Callahan, Guy Anderson, and Morris Graves—but the range and breadth of Pacific Northwest art goes beyond the “Big Four” of the Northwest School. Featuring more than 150 pieces, this exhibition showcases works that investigate the complexities of daily life in the region. Whether applying an ecocritical lens or producing dreamy and uncanny visuals, these artists expressed the duality of the natural world and city life to warn of the environmental and social impacts of industrialization. From Malcolm Roberts to Kenjiro Nomura, learn how Northwest artists applied their regional perspective to broader artistic developments to create a new form of American Modernism. The exhibition is accompanied by a fully illustrated catalogue authored by Theresa Papanikolas, SAM’s Ann M. Barwick Curator of American Art.

Whether you’re a student, family, community member, or volunteer, Connect for Good is the perfect place to support causes you care about, get involved in service, and strengthen our local community—all in one place. item.title: Make a difference for cats and dogs in need!
item.description:
Sunday, March 29, 2026 at 12 AM

Location: Pawsitive Alliance, 14150 NE 20th St. F1-233, Bellevue, WA

Are you interested in using your time, talent, and energy to support the vision of a healthy and happy home for every cat and dog in Washington? If yes, then please consider joining our Board! Pawsitive Alliance, a volunteer driven tax-exempt nonprofit organization, is having an open call for Board Members. Founded in 2005, the mission of Pawsitive Alliance is to help end the euthanasia of adoptable dogs and cats in Washington by increasing adoptions, supporting spay and neuter programs, and improving pet retention. Pawsitive Alliance advances are mission through our three programs: -  Adoption program: \#WhyNotMePets campaign, highlighting long-term homeless pets, and supporting adoption events to help homeless finds homes -  Spay/Neuter: providing vouchers and financial and logistical support to increase surgeries and access to end pet overpopulation and suffering -  Pet retention: collecting and distributing pet food and supplies and offering pet ID tags and microchips to keep pets thriving with their caretakers Our programs and activities prioritize increasing services for pets, people, and places across Washington state with few resources. Pawsitive Alliance’s biggest strengths are marketing, organizing, fundraising, and collaborating.  We work with more than 50 human and animal-welfare partners each year. Since 2005, we have helped over 8,000 animals find homes, delivered over 170,000 pounds of donated pet food, and facilitated more than 11,000 spay and neuter surgeries. The Pawsitive Alliance Board is a hands-on group of committed individuals, investing their time and resources to support our mission.  As we celebrate our 20th anniversary in 2025, and look to the future, we are looking to add individuals from diverse backgrounds to join the Board and help the organization’s continued success and growth.  Ideal candidates are committed to our mission, can commit at least 10-15 per month to the organization, are able to represent the organization in the community, are ready to engage in strategy setting, fundraising and networking, and bring a well-rounded set of skills to a unique and hard-working team.​ Pawsitive Alliance is always interested in bringing greater diversity and new areas of expertise, energy, and skills to the Board. For more information on the organization, refer to our website at www.pawsitivealliance.org.  Please fill out our Board Application if you share our passion for helping to end pet homelessness in Washington and are interested in serving and helping end pet overpopulation. Those interested in applying should email our Board Recruitment Committee at info@pawsitivealliance.org.

Event details

item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Sunday, March 29, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

From SEAtoday - Community & Civic

item.description:
Sunday, March 29, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

In Viewpoint, SAM Gallery artists take us down mysterious city streets, welcome us to green spaces, and inspire us to explore surreal landscapes. Featuring the work of Katie Metz, Elizabeth Gahan, Perri Lynch Howard and more. Join us and our featured artists for an opening reception on Thursday, March 5, 5:30-7:30 pm.

Event details

item.title: Samantha Yun Wall: What We Leave Behind
item.description:
Sunday, March 29, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Samantha Yun Wall’s new paintings use overlapping silhouettes of female figures as portals to unknown spaces and different temporal realms. Impetus for the new body of work is a Korean folk tale in which the Pasque flower is symbolic of a grandmother who passed away without the loving care of her grandchildren. It is a story of melancholy, loss, and remembrance. The delicate hair on the flower’s stem differentiates it, and the artist gives the plant a surreal aspect in some of her paintings, replacing the flower’s center with a watchful eye.

From SEAtoday - Community & Civic

Event details

item.title: Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light
item.description:
Sunday, March 29, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light invites you to become part of the art. Agha, a Pakistani American artist, animates spaces with her large-scale sculptural installations. Suspended from the ceiling, Agha’s steel cubes are laser-cut with intricate designs that project geometric shadows onto the visitor. She draws on both the light and dark of her own life, using South Asian art practices to convey the gender discrimination she faced growing up as a young girl in Pakistan. An in-depth exploration of Islamic architecture, art, and identity, this is the first solo exhibition of a Pakistani American artist in SAM’s 90-year history. Agha is carving a name out for herself in the art world—visit the Seattle Asian Art Museum for an immersive and illuminating experience.

From SEAtoday - Community & Civic

Event details

item.title: Kameelah Janan Rasheed: we leak, we exceed
item.description:
Sunday, March 29, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Henry Art Gallery, 15th Ave NE & 41st St, Seattle, WA

Kameelah Janan Rasheed \(b. 1985, East Palo Alto, CA; lives and works in Brooklyn, NY\) is a self-described “learner” who creates multi-media works that explore the poetics-pleasures-politics of Black knowledge production, information technologies, \[un\]learning, and belief formation. She often integrates found images and words alongside her own writing to create collaged wall installations and experimental video works. Across her work, Rasheed investigates the revision and life cycles of text, embracing expanded and embodied forms of writing and reading. we leak, we exceed will activate the unique volume and multiple vantage points of the Henry’s double-height gallery, drawing together threads from physics, Black critical thought, and information theory to create an immersive environment that interrogates the spatial and social implications of compression. A common process used in data storage, spatial organization, and information systems, compression abbreviates and collapses complex ideas into more simplified forms. Rasheed questions the way compression comes at the cost of nuance and creates unrecoverable losses. She draws parallels between the compression of information and the containment of people, both physically and through the structuring and defining of identities. Through a network of video, sound, and architectural mark-making, Rasheed proposes alternatively what she calls “an embrace of Black excess and expansion” as a liberatory practice.

From SEAtoday - Community & Civic

Event details

item.title: Boren Banner Series: Camille Trautman
item.description:
Sunday, March 29, 2026 at 11 AM

Location: Frye Art Museum, 704 Terry Ave, Seattle, WA

Every photograph is a reminder that the act of framing is never neutral. In Camille Trautman’s first solo museum exhibition in their hometown, the Seattle-born Duwamish artist uses photography and video to challenge colonial narratives and counter Indigenous erasure. The exhibition presents selections from their ongoing series The North American LCD—spectral self-portraits staged in varied natural landscapes. Rooted in Trautman’s process of coming out as transgender, the images show the artist’s body partially obscured by large LCD screens. The series examines how both landscape photography and digital media can shape or distort cultural identity, often by promoting or denying visibility.

From SEAtoday - Community & Civic

item.title: Ecosocialism and Green New Deal Working Group
item.description:
Monday, March 30, 2026 at 8 PM

Location: Virtual\, WA\, United States

Join us biweekly for continued conversations about our vision and strategies for ecosocialist politics. Ecosocialism promotes democratic control over all institutions that impact life and the environment. A Green New Deal (GND) distills actions to address the climate crisis through a rapid and just transformation of these institutions. Questions? Please contact greennewdeal@seattledsa.org . \nRSVP here for zoom link.

item.title: Make a difference for cats and dogs in need!
item.description:
Tuesday, March 31, 2026 at 12 AM

Location: Pawsitive Alliance, 14150 NE 20th St. F1-233, Bellevue, WA

Are you interested in using your time, talent, and energy to support the vision of a healthy and happy home for every cat and dog in Washington? If yes, then please consider joining our Board! Pawsitive Alliance, a volunteer driven tax-exempt nonprofit organization, is having an open call for Board Members. Founded in 2005, the mission of Pawsitive Alliance is to help end the euthanasia of adoptable dogs and cats in Washington by increasing adoptions, supporting spay and neuter programs, and improving pet retention. Pawsitive Alliance advances are mission through our three programs: -  Adoption program: \#WhyNotMePets campaign, highlighting long-term homeless pets, and supporting adoption events to help homeless finds homes -  Spay/Neuter: providing vouchers and financial and logistical support to increase surgeries and access to end pet overpopulation and suffering -  Pet retention: collecting and distributing pet food and supplies and offering pet ID tags and microchips to keep pets thriving with their caretakers Our programs and activities prioritize increasing services for pets, people, and places across Washington state with few resources. Pawsitive Alliance’s biggest strengths are marketing, organizing, fundraising, and collaborating.  We work with more than 50 human and animal-welfare partners each year. Since 2005, we have helped over 8,000 animals find homes, delivered over 170,000 pounds of donated pet food, and facilitated more than 11,000 spay and neuter surgeries. The Pawsitive Alliance Board is a hands-on group of committed individuals, investing their time and resources to support our mission.  As we celebrate our 20th anniversary in 2025, and look to the future, we are looking to add individuals from diverse backgrounds to join the Board and help the organization’s continued success and growth.  Ideal candidates are committed to our mission, can commit at least 10-15 per month to the organization, are able to represent the organization in the community, are ready to engage in strategy setting, fundraising and networking, and bring a well-rounded set of skills to a unique and hard-working team.​ Pawsitive Alliance is always interested in bringing greater diversity and new areas of expertise, energy, and skills to the Board. For more information on the organization, refer to our website at www.pawsitivealliance.org.  Please fill out our Board Application if you share our passion for helping to end pet homelessness in Washington and are interested in serving and helping end pet overpopulation. Those interested in applying should email our Board Recruitment Committee at info@pawsitivealliance.org.

item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Tuesday, March 31, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

item.title: Beyond Mysticism: The Modern Northwest
item.description:
Wednesday, April 1, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

image: Drift No. 2, 1936, Malcolm M. Roberts, American, 1913-1990, tempera on board, 25 1/2 x 20 5/8 in., Seattle Art Museum, Eugene Fuller Memorial Collection, 37.103, © Malcolm M. Roberts, photo: Scott Leen. Inspired by the region’s natural beauty and rapidly changing city, this exhibition showcases artists who told the raw story of Seattle in the 20th century. By sharing the Pacific Northwest’s perspective through trending art styles—including Social Realism, Surrealism, and Abstract Expressionism—these artists offered a unique take on Modernist art and challenged the “mystic” label ascribed to their works. Spearheading the movement were local icons Mark Tobey, Kenneth Callahan, Guy Anderson, and Morris Graves—but the range and breadth of Pacific Northwest art goes beyond the “Big Four” of the Northwest School. Featuring more than 150 pieces, this exhibition showcases works that investigate the complexities of daily life in the region. Whether applying an ecocritical lens or producing dreamy and uncanny visuals, these artists expressed the duality of the natural world and city life to warn of the environmental and social impacts of industrialization. From Malcolm Roberts to Kenjiro Nomura, learn how Northwest artists applied their regional perspective to broader artistic developments to create a new form of American Modernism. The exhibition is accompanied by a fully illustrated catalogue authored by Theresa Papanikolas, SAM’s Ann M. Barwick Curator of American Art.

In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. Event details

item.title: Samantha Yun Wall: What We Leave Behind
item.description:
Wednesday, April 1, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Samantha Yun Wall’s new paintings use overlapping silhouettes of female figures as portals to unknown spaces and different temporal realms. Impetus for the new body of work is a Korean folk tale in which the Pasque flower is symbolic of a grandmother who passed away without the loving care of her grandchildren. It is a story of melancholy, loss, and remembrance. The delicate hair on the flower’s stem differentiates it, and the artist gives the plant a surreal aspect in some of her paintings, replacing the flower’s center with a watchful eye.

From SEAtoday - Community & Civic

Event details

item.title: Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light
item.description:
Wednesday, April 1, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light invites you to become part of the art. Agha, a Pakistani American artist, animates spaces with her large-scale sculptural installations. Suspended from the ceiling, Agha’s steel cubes are laser-cut with intricate designs that project geometric shadows onto the visitor. She draws on both the light and dark of her own life, using South Asian art practices to convey the gender discrimination she faced growing up as a young girl in Pakistan. An in-depth exploration of Islamic architecture, art, and identity, this is the first solo exhibition of a Pakistani American artist in SAM’s 90-year history. Agha is carving a name out for herself in the art world—visit the Seattle Asian Art Museum for an immersive and illuminating experience.

From SEAtoday - Community & Civic

Event details

item.title: Boren Banner Series: Camille Trautman
item.description:
Wednesday, April 1, 2026 at 11 AM

Location: Frye Art Museum, 704 Terry Ave, Seattle, WA

Every photograph is a reminder that the act of framing is never neutral. In Camille Trautman’s first solo museum exhibition in their hometown, the Seattle-born Duwamish artist uses photography and video to challenge colonial narratives and counter Indigenous erasure. The exhibition presents selections from their ongoing series The North American LCD—spectral self-portraits staged in varied natural landscapes. Rooted in Trautman’s process of coming out as transgender, the images show the artist’s body partially obscured by large LCD screens. The series examines how both landscape photography and digital media can shape or distort cultural identity, often by promoting or denying visibility.

item.title: Screen Printing Open Session
item.description:
Wednesday, April 1, 2026 at 4 PM

Location: Book-It Repertory Theatre, 305 Harrison Street, Seattle, WA

You MUST take the SP 101: Intro to Vera's Screen Print Studio class before you can sign up for an Open Session\\\*\*\* Vera’s screen print studio offers four-hour Open Sessions where individuals who have completed the introductory class may use our screen print lab to work on their own projects. Our Open Sessions are looked over by a volunteer moderator, who can provide you with advice and guidance for anything taught in our Screen Printing 101 class. We offer three different kinds of tickets at Open Sessions: Vacuum Table for paper and other flat media, T-shirt Press for shirts, tote bags and other fabrics, and Screen prep for just screen preparation. Each ticket is limited to two. If the ticket you are looking for is sold out, please add yourself to the waitlist so you can be notified when a ticket becomes available. For Open Sessions, Vera provides: - Rentable screens - Emulsion and emulsion remover - Squegees - Basic tools like spatulas, rulers, tape, etc. Participants must provide their own: - Substrate \(paper, t-shirts, etc\) - Water based ink Vera \*does not\* allow the use of non-water based inks at our studio, as our studio is not set up to safely process non-water based ink. If you have any questions, please email matt@theveraproject.org. \*\*FAQ\*\* 1. Can Vera print a transparent stencil for me? Yes — You must send us your stencil at least 24 hours before the start of your session. Your file must be a .PDF, no larger than 12" x 18", and ready to print. Vera cannot make any edits to your file. Stencils are $5 each and can be paid via Debit/Credit when you arrive. If you are not interested in getting a stencil printed at Vera, you can print your own on transparency at a FedEx or similar print service location. 2. What if I need direct assistance on my projects? Our moderators are trained to assist with any topics covered in the SP 101 class offered at Vera. While some may be able to give you basic advice on your project, they are not expected to ask as a direct instructor. If you have a project where you would like one-on-one guidance, please reach out to matt@theveraproject.org to request private tutoring. 3. I really want to use your studio during an Open Session, but there are no slots available? Our studio is in high demand, so we apologize if our Open Session is sold out — We have limited space in the studio and several events every day at Vera, so it can be difficult for us to find more time for Open Sessions. If you're interested in booking the studio outside of Open Sessions, please email matt@theveraproject.org to discuss private rental rates and availability.

item.title: Make a difference for cats and dogs in need!
item.description:
Thursday, April 2, 2026 at 12 AM

Location: Pawsitive Alliance, 14150 NE 20th St. F1-233, Bellevue, WA

Are you interested in using your time, talent, and energy to support the vision of a healthy and happy home for every cat and dog in Washington? If yes, then please consider joining our Board! Pawsitive Alliance, a volunteer driven tax-exempt nonprofit organization, is having an open call for Board Members. Founded in 2005, the mission of Pawsitive Alliance is to help end the euthanasia of adoptable dogs and cats in Washington by increasing adoptions, supporting spay and neuter programs, and improving pet retention. Pawsitive Alliance advances are mission through our three programs: -  Adoption program: \#WhyNotMePets campaign, highlighting long-term homeless pets, and supporting adoption events to help homeless finds homes -  Spay/Neuter: providing vouchers and financial and logistical support to increase surgeries and access to end pet overpopulation and suffering -  Pet retention: collecting and distributing pet food and supplies and offering pet ID tags and microchips to keep pets thriving with their caretakers Our programs and activities prioritize increasing services for pets, people, and places across Washington state with few resources. Pawsitive Alliance’s biggest strengths are marketing, organizing, fundraising, and collaborating.  We work with more than 50 human and animal-welfare partners each year. Since 2005, we have helped over 8,000 animals find homes, delivered over 170,000 pounds of donated pet food, and facilitated more than 11,000 spay and neuter surgeries. The Pawsitive Alliance Board is a hands-on group of committed individuals, investing their time and resources to support our mission.  As we celebrate our 20th anniversary in 2025, and look to the future, we are looking to add individuals from diverse backgrounds to join the Board and help the organization’s continued success and growth.  Ideal candidates are committed to our mission, can commit at least 10-15 per month to the organization, are able to represent the organization in the community, are ready to engage in strategy setting, fundraising and networking, and bring a well-rounded set of skills to a unique and hard-working team.​ Pawsitive Alliance is always interested in bringing greater diversity and new areas of expertise, energy, and skills to the Board. For more information on the organization, refer to our website at www.pawsitivealliance.org.  Please fill out our Board Application if you share our passion for helping to end pet homelessness in Washington and are interested in serving and helping end pet overpopulation. Those interested in applying should email our Board Recruitment Committee at info@pawsitivealliance.org.

From SEAtoday - Community & Civic

Event details

item.title: Moomins' Sea Adventures & Tove and the Sea
item.description:
Thursday, April 2, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: National Nordic Museum, 2655 NW Market Street, Seattle, WA

Dive into the whimsical world of Moominvalley and the life of its creator, Tove Jansson, with *Moomins’ Sea Adventures* and *Tove and the Sea*. *Moomins’ Sea Adventures* brings Moominvalley to life with illustrations and life-sized characters from the stories. In the center of the exhibit is an interactive island complete with a lighthouse and rowboats that invites visitors to imagine adventures of their own. *Tove and the Sea* offers a more intimate counterpoint, tracing Tove Jansson’s lifelong relationship with the Finnish archipelago through large-scale photographs and reflective texts.

From SEAtoday - Community & Civic

Event details

item.title: Figure/Ground: New Criteria
item.description:
Thursday, April 2, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Henry Art Gallery, 15th Ave NE & 41st St, Seattle, WA

Figure/Ground: New Criteria is inspired by cultural historian Sarah Lewis’s concept of “groundwork aesthetics,” exploring figure-ground relationships and their connection to social tension. It builds on Lewis’s work, which—set against the expansion of Stand Your Ground laws across the United States since 2005, laws that broadly define rights to use lethal self-defense against perceived threats—describes a range of artistic interventions that reimagine “the literal and figurative meaning of ground.” Primarily featuring works from the Henry collection created in the twenty-first century, Figure/Ground reflects a period in which hard-won civil rights and claims to self-determination have been eroded across the US, disproportionately affecting Black, Brown, LGBTQ\+, and other marginalized communities.

From SEAtoday - Community & Civic

Event details

item.title: Beyond Mysticism: The Modern Northwest
item.description:
Thursday, April 2, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

image: Drift No. 2, 1936, Malcolm M. Roberts, American, 1913-1990, tempera on board, 25 1/2 x 20 5/8 in., Seattle Art Museum, Eugene Fuller Memorial Collection, 37.103, © Malcolm M. Roberts, photo: Scott Leen. Inspired by the region’s natural beauty and rapidly changing city, this exhibition showcases artists who told the raw story of Seattle in the 20th century. By sharing the Pacific Northwest’s perspective through trending art styles—including Social Realism, Surrealism, and Abstract Expressionism—these artists offered a unique take on Modernist art and challenged the “mystic” label ascribed to their works. Spearheading the movement were local icons Mark Tobey, Kenneth Callahan, Guy Anderson, and Morris Graves—but the range and breadth of Pacific Northwest art goes beyond the “Big Four” of the Northwest School. Featuring more than 150 pieces, this exhibition showcases works that investigate the complexities of daily life in the region. Whether applying an ecocritical lens or producing dreamy and uncanny visuals, these artists expressed the duality of the natural world and city life to warn of the environmental and social impacts of industrialization. From Malcolm Roberts to Kenjiro Nomura, learn how Northwest artists applied their regional perspective to broader artistic developments to create a new form of American Modernism. The exhibition is accompanied by a fully illustrated catalogue authored by Theresa Papanikolas, SAM’s Ann M. Barwick Curator of American Art.

From SEAtoday - Community & Civic

Event details

item.title: Eric-Paul Riege at Burke Artist Studio
item.description:
Thursday, April 2, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Henry Art Gallery, 15th Ave NE & 41st St, Seattle, WA

To celebrate the opening weekend of Eric-Paul Riege: ojo|-|ólǫ́, visit the artist offsite at the Burke Artist Studio, located in the Northwest Native Art Gallery at the Burke Museum, just a few blocks from the Henry. As part of Free First Thursday at the Burke, visitors will have the chance to watch the artist at work and speak with Riege about his process. ojo|-|ólǫ́ is based on Eric-Paul Riege's material research and engagement with the Navajo collections held by the Burke Museum, as well as the Brown University's Haffenreffer Museum of Anthropology. The resulting new body of work is informed by ancestral knowledge and traditions contained within Indigenous objects, and which aims, in part, to redress practices of cultural dispossession by museums and other institutions. Bio Eric-Paul Riege \(Diné\) is a weaver and fiber artist working in collage, durational performance, installation, woven sculpture, and wearable art. Using weaving as both means and metaphor to tell hybrid tales that interlace stories from Diné spirituality with his own interpretations and cosmology, he understands his artworks as animate and mobile. His practice pays homage and links him to generations of weavers in his family who aid him in generating spaces of sanctuary. Riege's recent solo exhibitions include iiZiiT \[3\]: RIEGE Jewelry \+ Supply at Canal Projects, New York \(2025\), Hammer Projects: Eric Paul Riege at the Hammer Museum, Los Angeles \(2022-2023\), and Hól ́ǫ-it xistz at the Institute of Contemporary Art Miami \(2019\). His recent group exhibitions include the 24th Biennale of Sydney in Australia \(2024\), Indian Theater: Native Performance, Art, and Self-Determination \(2023\), Prospect.5 Triennial in New Orleans \(2022\), and the Toronto Biennial of Art \(2022\). He holds a BFA in Art Studio and Ecology from the University of New Mexico in Albuquerque. His work is collected by Forge Project and ICA Miami, among others. He is represented by Bockley Gallery \(MN\) and STARS Gallery \(CA\). Riege is a member of the Charcoal Streaked Division of the Red Running Into the Water clan. He was born and is based in Na'nízhoozhí \[Gallup, New Mexico\].

From SEAtoday - Community & Civic

Event details

item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Thursday, April 2, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

From SEAtoday - Community & Civic

Event details

item.title: Samantha Yun Wall: What We Leave Behind
item.description:
Thursday, April 2, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Samantha Yun Wall’s new paintings use overlapping silhouettes of female figures as portals to unknown spaces and different temporal realms. Impetus for the new body of work is a Korean folk tale in which the Pasque flower is symbolic of a grandmother who passed away without the loving care of her grandchildren. It is a story of melancholy, loss, and remembrance. The delicate hair on the flower’s stem differentiates it, and the artist gives the plant a surreal aspect in some of her paintings, replacing the flower’s center with a watchful eye.

From SEAtoday - Community & Civic

item.title: Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light
item.description:
Thursday, April 2, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light invites you to become part of the art. Agha, a Pakistani American artist, animates spaces with her large-scale sculptural installations. Suspended from the ceiling, Agha’s steel cubes are laser-cut with intricate designs that project geometric shadows onto the visitor. She draws on both the light and dark of her own life, using South Asian art practices to convey the gender discrimination she faced growing up as a young girl in Pakistan. An in-depth exploration of Islamic architecture, art, and identity, this is the first solo exhibition of a Pakistani American artist in SAM’s 90-year history. Agha is carving a name out for herself in the art world—visit the Seattle Asian Art Museum for an immersive and illuminating experience.

From SEAtoday - Community & Civic

Event details

item.title: Kameelah Janan Rasheed: we leak, we exceed
item.description:
Thursday, April 2, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Henry Art Gallery, 15th Ave NE & 41st St, Seattle, WA

Kameelah Janan Rasheed \(b. 1985, East Palo Alto, CA; lives and works in Brooklyn, NY\) is a self-described “learner” who creates multi-media works that explore the poetics-pleasures-politics of Black knowledge production, information technologies, \[un\]learning, and belief formation. She often integrates found images and words alongside her own writing to create collaged wall installations and experimental video works. Across her work, Rasheed investigates the revision and life cycles of text, embracing expanded and embodied forms of writing and reading. we leak, we exceed will activate the unique volume and multiple vantage points of the Henry’s double-height gallery, drawing together threads from physics, Black critical thought, and information theory to create an immersive environment that interrogates the spatial and social implications of compression. A common process used in data storage, spatial organization, and information systems, compression abbreviates and collapses complex ideas into more simplified forms. Rasheed questions the way compression comes at the cost of nuance and creates unrecoverable losses. She draws parallels between the compression of information and the containment of people, both physically and through the structuring and defining of identities. Through a network of video, sound, and architectural mark-making, Rasheed proposes alternatively what she calls “an embrace of Black excess and expansion” as a liberatory practice.

From SEAtoday - Community & Civic

item.title: Boren Banner Series: Camille Trautman
item.description:
Thursday, April 2, 2026 at 11 AM

Location: Frye Art Museum, 704 Terry Ave, Seattle, WA

Every photograph is a reminder that the act of framing is never neutral. In Camille Trautman’s first solo museum exhibition in their hometown, the Seattle-born Duwamish artist uses photography and video to challenge colonial narratives and counter Indigenous erasure. The exhibition presents selections from their ongoing series The North American LCD—spectral self-portraits staged in varied natural landscapes. Rooted in Trautman’s process of coming out as transgender, the images show the artist’s body partially obscured by large LCD screens. The series examines how both landscape photography and digital media can shape or distort cultural identity, often by promoting or denying visibility.

item.title: Scott Gibson - Visual Symphony II
item.description:
Thursday, April 2, 2026 at 12 PM

Location: Gray Sky Gallery, 320 First Ave. S., Seattle, WA

In March, Visual Symphony II Scott Gibson invites viewers into a world where music and art collide. Each work is an exploration of the vivid, sensory experience of sound translating into vibrant, emotional visuals. As an artist with synesthesia each work expresses Scott’s personal perception of what music looks like. Scott uses resin techniques that he has pioneered and pushes the boundaries of this medium.

item.title: LANDSCAPE TECHNICIAN BOOTCAMP
item.description:
Friday, April 3, 2026 at 7 AM

Location: Lake Washington Institute of Technology - LWTech, 11605 132nd Ave NE, Kirkland, WA

LANDSCAPE TECHNICIAN BOOTCAMP Friday, April 3, 2026 Lake Washington Institute of Technology – 11605 132nd Avenue NE, Kirkland, WA 98034-8506 TITLE SPONSOR - DURANTA The Washington Association of Landscape Professionals is excited to offer the LANDSCAPE TECHNICIAN BOOTCAMP training in partnership with the National Association of Landscape Professionals. WALP will be offering the LANDSCAPE TECHNICIAN BOOTCAMP in APRIL 3, 2026 at Lake Washington Insitute of Technology that will be comprehensive training for your staff and workforce. Hands-On Training For Your Crews! The new Landscape Technician Boot Camp will provide hands-on training for your crews. Based on the curriculum for the Landscape Industry Certified Technician Exterior exam, this industry-vetted training will help prepare your crews to work more effectively in 2026. Boot Camp includes training in techniques and safety in 15 different competency areas organized into 3 disciplines presented in Spanish and English! Bonus! This course also helps prepare people for the Landscape Industry Certified Technician Exterior exam. So, once you’ve trained your team, consider registering them to become certified through the National Association of Landscape Professionals \(NALP\). Registration Costs: WALP Members \(First 5 registrants\): $95.00 per person WALP Members \(6\+ registrants\): $80.00 per person NON-WALP Members : $145.00 per person

Interested in exploring what a labor-focused approach to winning trans rights might look like? Come help brainstorm and sketch out the early phases of our Unionize for Trans Rights​ campaign! \nWe’ll be at Big Time Brewery​ in the U District for a working-social where we’ll discuss how we can help trans and nonbinary workers organize their workplaces and enshrine protections into union contracts. This is also an opportunity for those looking to get involved with this campaign to get to know one another—we hope you’ll join us! \nRSVP here!

item.title: Scott Gibson - Visual Symphony II
item.description:
Friday, April 3, 2026 at 12 PM

Location: Gray Sky Gallery, 320 First Ave. S., Seattle, WA

In March, Visual Symphony II Scott Gibson invites viewers into a world where music and art collide. Each work is an exploration of the vivid, sensory experience of sound translating into vibrant, emotional visuals. As an artist with synesthesia each work expresses Scott’s personal perception of what music looks like. Scott uses resin techniques that he has pioneered and pushes the boundaries of this medium.

item.title: Make a difference for cats and dogs in need!
item.description:
Saturday, April 4, 2026 at 12 AM

Location: Pawsitive Alliance, 14150 NE 20th St. F1-233, Bellevue, WA

Are you interested in using your time, talent, and energy to support the vision of a healthy and happy home for every cat and dog in Washington? If yes, then please consider joining our Board! Pawsitive Alliance, a volunteer driven tax-exempt nonprofit organization, is having an open call for Board Members. Founded in 2005, the mission of Pawsitive Alliance is to help end the euthanasia of adoptable dogs and cats in Washington by increasing adoptions, supporting spay and neuter programs, and improving pet retention. Pawsitive Alliance advances are mission through our three programs: -  Adoption program: \#WhyNotMePets campaign, highlighting long-term homeless pets, and supporting adoption events to help homeless finds homes -  Spay/Neuter: providing vouchers and financial and logistical support to increase surgeries and access to end pet overpopulation and suffering -  Pet retention: collecting and distributing pet food and supplies and offering pet ID tags and microchips to keep pets thriving with their caretakers Our programs and activities prioritize increasing services for pets, people, and places across Washington state with few resources. Pawsitive Alliance’s biggest strengths are marketing, organizing, fundraising, and collaborating.  We work with more than 50 human and animal-welfare partners each year. Since 2005, we have helped over 8,000 animals find homes, delivered over 170,000 pounds of donated pet food, and facilitated more than 11,000 spay and neuter surgeries. The Pawsitive Alliance Board is a hands-on group of committed individuals, investing their time and resources to support our mission.  As we celebrate our 20th anniversary in 2025, and look to the future, we are looking to add individuals from diverse backgrounds to join the Board and help the organization’s continued success and growth.  Ideal candidates are committed to our mission, can commit at least 10-15 per month to the organization, are able to represent the organization in the community, are ready to engage in strategy setting, fundraising and networking, and bring a well-rounded set of skills to a unique and hard-working team.​ Pawsitive Alliance is always interested in bringing greater diversity and new areas of expertise, energy, and skills to the Board. For more information on the organization, refer to our website at www.pawsitivealliance.org.  Please fill out our Board Application if you share our passion for helping to end pet homelessness in Washington and are interested in serving and helping end pet overpopulation. Those interested in applying should email our Board Recruitment Committee at info@pawsitivealliance.org.

item.title: Journaling the Garden
item.description:
Saturday, April 4, 2026 at 9 AM

Location: Bellevue Botanical Garden, 12001 Main St, Bellevue, WA

Nature journaling at the Bellevue Botanical Garden is a wonderful way to connect with the environment through curiosity and observation of the natural world. This free adult program will help guide your practice whether you are just starting out or are an experienced journaler. In this warm and welcoming environment, you will be encouraged to experiment and find your voice. In each session we will gather for introductions and brief guidance, and then head out into Garden to journal and come back together to share experiences.   Please bring a dedicated journal and something to write with. We will meet at 9am the first Saturday of January-November 2026. Registration is recommended but not required.  This free adult program is facilitated by Anne Dziok, Adult Education Program Manager. Anne is a practicing multi-media artist who shows her work regionally.    Email us  with questions.

item.title: Moomins' Sea Adventures & Tove and the Sea
item.description:
Saturday, April 4, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: National Nordic Museum, 2655 NW Market Street, Seattle, WA

Dive into the whimsical world of Moominvalley and the life of its creator, Tove Jansson, with *Moomins’ Sea Adventures* and *Tove and the Sea*. *Moomins’ Sea Adventures* brings Moominvalley to life with illustrations and life-sized characters from the stories. In the center of the exhibit is an interactive island complete with a lighthouse and rowboats that invites visitors to imagine adventures of their own. *Tove and the Sea* offers a more intimate counterpoint, tracing Tove Jansson’s lifelong relationship with the Finnish archipelago through large-scale photographs and reflective texts.

item.title: Figure/Ground: New Criteria
item.description:
Saturday, April 4, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Henry Art Gallery, 15th Ave NE & 41st St, Seattle, WA

Figure/Ground: New Criteria is inspired by cultural historian Sarah Lewis’s concept of “groundwork aesthetics,” exploring figure-ground relationships and their connection to social tension. It builds on Lewis’s work, which—set against the expansion of Stand Your Ground laws across the United States since 2005, laws that broadly define rights to use lethal self-defense against perceived threats—describes a range of artistic interventions that reimagine “the literal and figurative meaning of ground.” Primarily featuring works from the Henry collection created in the twenty-first century, Figure/Ground reflects a period in which hard-won civil rights and claims to self-determination have been eroded across the US, disproportionately affecting Black, Brown, LGBTQ\+, and other marginalized communities.

From SEAtoday - Community & Civic

Event details

item.title: Beyond Mysticism: The Modern Northwest
item.description:
Saturday, April 4, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

image: Drift No. 2, 1936, Malcolm M. Roberts, American, 1913-1990, tempera on board, 25 1/2 x 20 5/8 in., Seattle Art Museum, Eugene Fuller Memorial Collection, 37.103, © Malcolm M. Roberts, photo: Scott Leen. Inspired by the region’s natural beauty and rapidly changing city, this exhibition showcases artists who told the raw story of Seattle in the 20th century. By sharing the Pacific Northwest’s perspective through trending art styles—including Social Realism, Surrealism, and Abstract Expressionism—these artists offered a unique take on Modernist art and challenged the “mystic” label ascribed to their works. Spearheading the movement were local icons Mark Tobey, Kenneth Callahan, Guy Anderson, and Morris Graves—but the range and breadth of Pacific Northwest art goes beyond the “Big Four” of the Northwest School. Featuring more than 150 pieces, this exhibition showcases works that investigate the complexities of daily life in the region. Whether applying an ecocritical lens or producing dreamy and uncanny visuals, these artists expressed the duality of the natural world and city life to warn of the environmental and social impacts of industrialization. From Malcolm Roberts to Kenjiro Nomura, learn how Northwest artists applied their regional perspective to broader artistic developments to create a new form of American Modernism. The exhibition is accompanied by a fully illustrated catalogue authored by Theresa Papanikolas, SAM’s Ann M. Barwick Curator of American Art.

item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Saturday, April 4, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

From SEAtoday - Community & Civic

item.title: Earth Month Event | Restoration at Gallagher Hill Open Space
item.description:
Saturday, April 4, 2026 at 10 AM

Celebrate Earth Month through Volunteering in your Local Park! Gallagher Hill Open Space is a beautiful park with old trees, a thriving stream, and plenty of native plant and animal life. A lot of the park is unfortunately being overgrown with blackberry and ivy, threating the health of this vital ecosystem. Situated above the freeway Gallagher Hil l is an important respite of nature that is well worth protecting. Come jo in us for a park clean-up where we will be removing ivy. It will be a grea t chance to get your hands in the dirt, learn about native plants and our local forests, and work alongside neighbors and friends! No experience necessary and all ages welcome; gloves and tools are provided. Please note: we require participants aged 14 and under to volunteer with a pare nt or guardian at a ratio of 1 adult to 3 kids. When: Saturday, April 4th, 10am-1pm Where: Gallagher Hill Open Space off of Shorewood Dr, follow VOLUNTEER EVENT Signs What to bring: Dress for the weather in cloth es you don't mind getting dirty \(think: long pants, boots or sneakers\). Bring a water bottle. Small snacks, gloves, and tools will be provided. Registration: https://volunteersignup.org/KMTKY

item.title: Samantha Yun Wall: What We Leave Behind
item.description:
Saturday, April 4, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Samantha Yun Wall’s new paintings use overlapping silhouettes of female figures as portals to unknown spaces and different temporal realms. Impetus for the new body of work is a Korean folk tale in which the Pasque flower is symbolic of a grandmother who passed away without the loving care of her grandchildren. It is a story of melancholy, loss, and remembrance. The delicate hair on the flower’s stem differentiates it, and the artist gives the plant a surreal aspect in some of her paintings, replacing the flower’s center with a watchful eye.

Event details

item.title: Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light
item.description:
Saturday, April 4, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light invites you to become part of the art. Agha, a Pakistani American artist, animates spaces with her large-scale sculptural installations. Suspended from the ceiling, Agha’s steel cubes are laser-cut with intricate designs that project geometric shadows onto the visitor. She draws on both the light and dark of her own life, using South Asian art practices to convey the gender discrimination she faced growing up as a young girl in Pakistan. An in-depth exploration of Islamic architecture, art, and identity, this is the first solo exhibition of a Pakistani American artist in SAM’s 90-year history. Agha is carving a name out for herself in the art world—visit the Seattle Asian Art Museum for an immersive and illuminating experience.

From SEAtoday - Community & Civic

Event details

item.title: Kameelah Janan Rasheed: we leak, we exceed
item.description:
Saturday, April 4, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Henry Art Gallery, 15th Ave NE & 41st St, Seattle, WA

Kameelah Janan Rasheed \(b. 1985, East Palo Alto, CA; lives and works in Brooklyn, NY\) is a self-described “learner” who creates multi-media works that explore the poetics-pleasures-politics of Black knowledge production, information technologies, \[un\]learning, and belief formation. She often integrates found images and words alongside her own writing to create collaged wall installations and experimental video works. Across her work, Rasheed investigates the revision and life cycles of text, embracing expanded and embodied forms of writing and reading. we leak, we exceed will activate the unique volume and multiple vantage points of the Henry’s double-height gallery, drawing together threads from physics, Black critical thought, and information theory to create an immersive environment that interrogates the spatial and social implications of compression. A common process used in data storage, spatial organization, and information systems, compression abbreviates and collapses complex ideas into more simplified forms. Rasheed questions the way compression comes at the cost of nuance and creates unrecoverable losses. She draws parallels between the compression of information and the containment of people, both physically and through the structuring and defining of identities. Through a network of video, sound, and architectural mark-making, Rasheed proposes alternatively what she calls “an embrace of Black excess and expansion” as a liberatory practice.

From SEAtoday - Community & Civic

Event details

item.title: Spring Educator Workshop: Seeing Self and Place in the Modern Northwest
item.description:
Saturday, April 4, 2026 at 10:30 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Explore the symbolic and deeply personal visions of artists who helped define a unique modernist movement in the Pacific Northwest. Centering Beyond Mysticism: The Modern Northwest, educators will gain insight on how artists blended abstraction and nature to express complex ideas about identity, place, and purpose. Participants will engage in a studio art activity inspired by the exhibition, explore student-led inquiry models, and reflect and connect with fellow educators. Tickets This workshop is free to attend, but reservations are required. This workshop includes resources and Washington State Clock Hours. K-12 educators, paraeducators, subject area coaches, and administrators are welcome to attend. Support for K-12 programs during the 2025-2026 school year is provided by the Foster Foundation. Partial, sustained support for SAM's K-12 programs is provided by an endowment established in 1999 by a National Endowment for the Humanities challenge grant and the generous contributions of matching donors, including the Ann P. Wyckoff Education Endowment. The William Randolph Hearst Endowed Fund for Education Programs at the Seattle Art Museum has supported SAM K-12 programs since 1994.

item.description:

Come play soccer with fellow socialists! This is a fun\, enjoyable way to get outdoors\, exercise\, and meet friendly socialists as well as non-socialist soccer enthusiasts! All genders and skill levels are welcome! Safety is our top priority. To participate you must abide by our norms and agreements to avoid injuries and help us take care of one another. Join us at Brighton Playfield in South Seattle. Map: goo.gl/maps/c74cytjrRB1vpT7h7 We almost always play on the most southern edge of the field. item.title: Seattle DSA Fundraising Drive
item.description:
Sunday, April 5, 2026 at 1 PM

Location: Beacon Hill Branch – Seattle Public Library\, 2821 Beacon Ave S\, Seattle\, WA\, 98144\, United States

Join us on Sunday\, April 5th as we make calls to Seattle DSA members and ask them to make monthly contributions to help close our $8\,500 budget gap this year! Seattle DSA has been working hard this year to protect our neighbors\, promote progressive taxes\, and fight for a better world. Closing our budget gap will allow us to keep it up\, and exceeding our goals would allow us to act even more boldly. Your friends and comrades are going to ask members who are currently giving monthly local donations to chip in a bit more each month. We’ll also be calling members who aren’t yet providing donations to the chapter and asking them to start. You’ll be provided a script and a list of people to call. If you’ve ever wondered how you can get people to buy into financially supporting grassroots projects\, this is a great place to start. Kameelah Janan Rasheed \(b. 1985, East Palo Alto, CA; lives and works in Brooklyn, NY\) is a self-described “learner” who creates multi-media works that explore the poetics-pleasures-politics of Black knowledge production, information technologies, \[un\]learning, and belief formation. item.title: Boren Banner Series: Camille Trautman
item.description:
Sunday, April 5, 2026 at 11 AM

Location: Frye Art Museum, 704 Terry Ave, Seattle, WA

Every photograph is a reminder that the act of framing is never neutral. In Camille Trautman’s first solo museum exhibition in their hometown, the Seattle-born Duwamish artist uses photography and video to challenge colonial narratives and counter Indigenous erasure. The exhibition presents selections from their ongoing series The North American LCD—spectral self-portraits staged in varied natural landscapes. Rooted in Trautman’s process of coming out as transgender, the images show the artist’s body partially obscured by large LCD screens. The series examines how both landscape photography and digital media can shape or distort cultural identity, often by promoting or denying visibility.

From SEAtoday - Community & Civic

In March, Visual Symphony II Scott Gibson invites viewers into a world where music and art collide. Each work is an exploration of the vivid, sensory experience of sound translating into vibrant, emotional visuals. As an artist with synesthesia each work expresses Scott’s personal perception of what music looks like. Scott uses resin techniques that he has pioneered and pushes the boundaries of this medium.

item.title: Make a difference for cats and dogs in need!
item.description:
Monday, April 6, 2026 at 12 AM

Location: Pawsitive Alliance, 14150 NE 20th St. F1-233, Bellevue, WA

Are you interested in using your time, talent, and energy to support the vision of a healthy and happy home for every cat and dog in Washington? If yes, then please consider joining our Board! Pawsitive Alliance, a volunteer driven tax-exempt nonprofit organization, is having an open call for Board Members. Founded in 2005, the mission of Pawsitive Alliance is to help end the euthanasia of adoptable dogs and cats in Washington by increasing adoptions, supporting spay and neuter programs, and improving pet retention. Pawsitive Alliance advances are mission through our three programs: -  Adoption program: \#WhyNotMePets campaign, highlighting long-term homeless pets, and supporting adoption events to help homeless finds homes -  Spay/Neuter: providing vouchers and financial and logistical support to increase surgeries and access to end pet overpopulation and suffering -  Pet retention: collecting and distributing pet food and supplies and offering pet ID tags and microchips to keep pets thriving with their caretakers Our programs and activities prioritize increasing services for pets, people, and places across Washington state with few resources. Pawsitive Alliance’s biggest strengths are marketing, organizing, fundraising, and collaborating.  We work with more than 50 human and animal-welfare partners each year. Since 2005, we have helped over 8,000 animals find homes, delivered over 170,000 pounds of donated pet food, and facilitated more than 11,000 spay and neuter surgeries. The Pawsitive Alliance Board is a hands-on group of committed individuals, investing their time and resources to support our mission.  As we celebrate our 20th anniversary in 2025, and look to the future, we are looking to add individuals from diverse backgrounds to join the Board and help the organization’s continued success and growth.  Ideal candidates are committed to our mission, can commit at least 10-15 per month to the organization, are able to represent the organization in the community, are ready to engage in strategy setting, fundraising and networking, and bring a well-rounded set of skills to a unique and hard-working team.​ Pawsitive Alliance is always interested in bringing greater diversity and new areas of expertise, energy, and skills to the Board. For more information on the organization, refer to our website at www.pawsitivealliance.org.  Please fill out our Board Application if you share our passion for helping to end pet homelessness in Washington and are interested in serving and helping end pet overpopulation. Those interested in applying should email our Board Recruitment Committee at info@pawsitivealliance.org.

Event details

item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Monday, April 6, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

item.title: Building a More Welcoming Washington: Seattle's FIFA World Cup Inclusion Training
item.description:
Monday, April 6, 2026 at 3 PM

Location: GSBA- Washington State&'s LGBTQ+ Chamber of Commerce, 400 E Pine St Ste 322, Seattle, WA

Description Inclusion Training Initiative: Building a More Welcoming Washington As Washington prepares to welcome the world for FIFA World Cup 2026, local businesses will be on the front lines of hospitality-serving visitors, supporting staff, and representing the values of our region every day. GSBA is partnering with SeattleFWC26 to offer Inclusion Training workshops designed to help small businesses and organizations feel prepared, confident, and welcoming. These sessions provide practical, real-world guidance on allyship, gender inclusion, and LGBTQ\+ cultural competency, with a focus on everyday customer and workplace interactions. About the Inclusion Training Initiative As part of SeattleFWC26's commitment to ensuring every fan, visitor, and resident feels welcome, the Inclusion Training Initiative-delivered in partnership with GSBA-equips local businesses and organizations with tools to create authentically inclusive environments for LGBTQ\+ customers, employees, and communities. About River Teslar River \(she/her\) is a trans woman and nonprofit professional with four years of experience at GSBA developing and facilitating LGBTQ\+ Inclusion Trainings through a social justice- and trauma-informed lens. Drawing from lived experience and community-based work, she creates learning spaces grounded in empathy, curiosity, and practical application, where participants feel supported engaging at any level. Key Activities ⚽ Four Inclusion Workshops: Hosted by GSBA and tailored for small businesses, nonprofits, and frontline teams. ⚽ Interactive Learning: Sessions focus on allyship, gender inclusion, and LGBTQ\+ cultural competency in the workplace, with real-world scenarios and discussion. ⚽ Accessible Participation: Two in-person workshops hosted at GSBA's Capitol Hill office \(or other community venues\) and two virtual workshops open to participants statewide. ⚽ Practical Tools: Participants receive actionable guidance and resources to help strengthen inclusive policies, communications, and customer experiences. Outcomes & Benefits ⚽ More Inclusive Businesses: Participating organizations gain the knowledge to create spaces that welcome all genders, identities, and relationships. ⚽ Community Visibility: Strengthens Washington's reputation as a leader in inclusive tourism and community hospitality ahead of FIFA World Cup 2026. ⚽ Economic Impact: Inclusive environments encourage repeat visitation, word-of-mouth referrals, and stronger customer loyalty. ⚽ Sustained Legacy: Builds long-term capacity for inclusion that extends beyond the tournament, leaving a lasting mark on Washington's business and tourism ecosystem. Workshop Schedule Virtual Workshops ⚽ Thursday, January 15 - 10:00 AM ⚽ Wednesday, March 4 - 3:00 PM In-Person Workshops ⚽ Monday, February 9 - 10:00 AM ⚽ Monday, April 6 - 3:00 PM Learn more about the initiative: SeattleFWC26 Inclusion Training Initiative

item.title: Moomins' Sea Adventures & Tove and the Sea
item.description:
Tuesday, April 7, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: National Nordic Museum, 2655 NW Market Street, Seattle, WA

Dive into the whimsical world of Moominvalley and the life of its creator, Tove Jansson, with *Moomins’ Sea Adventures* and *Tove and the Sea*. *Moomins’ Sea Adventures* brings Moominvalley to life with illustrations and life-sized characters from the stories. In the center of the exhibit is an interactive island complete with a lighthouse and rowboats that invites visitors to imagine adventures of their own. *Tove and the Sea* offers a more intimate counterpoint, tracing Tove Jansson’s lifelong relationship with the Finnish archipelago through large-scale photographs and reflective texts.
